item.title: China Procurement Agent
item.description: Our China procurement agency service helps overseas buyers connect with reliable manufacturers efficiently.

item.title: Ground Security Radar
item.description:
Ground security radar fits perimeter monitoring for campuses, bridges, oilfields, and industrial facilities, enabling persistent surveillance and anomaly alerts.

Typical Scenarios

  • Industrial and campus perimeter protection
  • Critical infrastructure monitoring
  • Border patrol and early warning

Delivery Scope

We support product selection, deployment design, on-site integration, and export logistics.

item.title: HD Laser Detection
item.description: HD laser detection modules deliver precise target confirmation and persistent tracking for critical monitoring tasks.

item.title: Perimeter Security
item.description: Perimeter consulting focuses on risk identification, alert process optimization, and cross-system coordination.

item.title: Radar-EO Integrated Unit
item.description: The radar-EO integrated unit improves alert accuracy through multi-source fusion and shortens implementation cycles.

item.title: SDC-PT-D02
item.description: SDC-PT-D02 is positioned as a portable RF detection node for projects that need more than simple signal presence awareness. It is intended for teams that want drone detection, pilot positioning, trajectory tracking, and practical integration with layered counter-UAS workflows.

item.title: SOC-D05G-1U
item.description: SOC-D05G-1U is the short-range member of the Cyrentis SOC line. It is designed for projects that need a lighter gyro-stabilized day-night payload with configurable daylight optics and an optional rangefinder, rather than the longer reach and heavier envelope of the cooled series.

item.title: Global Logistics Transport
item.description: Global logistics transport services ensure stable delivery from factory release to destination handover.

item.title: Long-range Infrared Detection
item.description: Long-range infrared detection is suitable for night patrol, harsh-weather sensing, and persistent target monitoring.

item.title: Low-altitude Counter-UAS Radar
item.description:
Low-altitude counter-UAS radar is designed for airports, energy facilities, and campuses, improving low-altitude situational awareness and risk response.

Typical Scenarios

  • Airport perimeter airspace protection
  • Event security operations
  • Energy and industrial base defense

item.title: Low-altitude Security
item.description: Low-altitude consulting covers detection, verification, and emergency response to establish reliable aerial defense capability.

item.title: SOC-D13G-2A
item.description: SOC-D13G-2A is positioned as a dual-spectrum observation payload for projects where an alert alone is not enough and the operator still needs readable confirmation. The visible and thermal channels are strong enough to support day-night target verification, while the gimbal architecture keeps the payload credible on rooftops, temporary field positions, and mobile platforms.

item.title: SOC-V06G-2U
item.description: SOC-V06G-2U brings a visible-only and laser-assisted option into the Cyrentis SOC library. It sits between compact dual-spectrum heads and the heavier cooled line, giving projects a dedicated long-zoom day-night camera where thermal sensing is not the primary purchase driver.

item.title: Unattended Security Outpost
item.description: The unattended security outpost enables persistent surveillance in remote zones with low maintenance overhead.

item.title: Bird Identification & Deterrence
item.description: Bird identification and deterrence consulting helps reduce bird-strike risk and improve operational safety in critical zones.

item.title: SOC-D20G-2U
item.description: SOC-D20G-2U is the long-range cooled member of the current Cyrentis SOC product line. It is intended for projects that need deeper identification distance, a configurable daylight package, and a stronger fit for coastal, shipborne, or large fixed-site deployment.

item.title: SOC-M13G-3U
item.description: SOC-M13G-3U expands the Cyrentis SOC library with a multi-band visible and thermal platform aimed at longer-watch, fire-aware, and integration-heavy deployments. It sits above the medium dual-spectrum tier when the site needs broader analytics, more alarm context, and optional sensor expansion inside one outdoor payload.

item.title: Choosing Radar Frequency Bands: Pros, Cons, and Application Scenarios
item.description: Civil security radar projects rarely fail because of one parameter. They fail when frequency band choice is disconnected from site conditions, target mix, and system integration objectives.

item.title: Power Modules
item.description: Power modules cover regulated supply, surge protection, and battery-friendly operation profiles for long-running security systems.

item.title: Security Software Customization
item.description: Security software customization at Cyrentis is centered on Horizon. The service adapts the platform to the way a project actually runs, including operator roles, interface structure, event handling, command flow, and device collaboration.

item.title: Comparison of Different Radar Scanning Architectures
item.description: In civil security radar deployment, architecture is not a cosmetic option. It determines coverage behavior, revisit pattern, mechanical wear profile, and how tracks are consumed by upper-layer command platforms.

item.title: Compliance Overview for Dual-Use Export of Civil Security Radar Products
item.description: Civil security radar projects can fail late in the delivery cycle if compliance planning starts too late. For cross-border deployment, technical readiness and regulatory readiness must progress in parallel.

item.title: Radar, LiDAR, Ultrasonic, and OTH Radar: Which Sensing Layer Solves Which Problem?
item.description: Security projects often go wrong at the first decision: sensors are compared as if they were substitutes, when in practice they are layers with different jobs. The right question is not “Which technology is best?” but “Which sensing layer solves which part of the mission?”

item.title: Chipsets
item.description: Our chipset offerings include compute, control, and interface solutions used in radar processing, EO data handling, and embedded security devices.

item.title: Security Solution Design
item.description: Security solution design turns a monitoring scenario into a deployable site architecture. Cyrentis defines how many radars and optics are needed, where they should be installed, how the data links should be designed, and how the control room should be configured around Horizon.

item.title: Radar System Components Explained: Front End, Back End, and Data Flow
item.description: When people say “radar,” they often picture a rotating antenna. In real projects, that is only the visible tip of the system. What matters operationally is the entire chain that generates a waveform, sends it into space, receives the echo, extracts useful meaning, and presents the result to an operator or command platform.

item.title: Synthetic Aperture Radar Explained: Principles, Imaging Modes, and Civil Applications
item.description: Synthetic Aperture Radar, usually shortened to SAR, is one of the most important sensing technologies for observing the Earth when optics cannot be trusted. It matters because it does not wait for daylight, clear sky, or ideal weather. It creates its own illumination, measures with microwaves, and can keep producing useful imagery when optical systems struggle.

item.title: From GaAs to GaN: What Makes AESA Radar Industrially Ready?
item.description: When people talk about modern electronically scanned radar, the discussion quickly turns to AESA, T/R modules, GaAs, and GaN. Those terms are important, but they are often used too loosely. The real question for an engineering team or buyer is not whether a supplier can say “AESA” or “GaN.” It is whether the radar has reached industrial maturity.

item.title: Why RF Digitization Is Reshaping Modern Radar Systems
item.description: RF digitization is one of the clearest signals that radar is no longer just an RF hardware business. It is increasingly a software, processing, and system-integration business as well.

item.title: Layered Radar Architectures: What Civil Security Planners Can Borrow from Long-, Mid-, and Short-Range Systems
item.description: Large radar ecosystems are often described in terms of long-range, mid-range, and short-range layers. Civil security teams do not need to copy that structure literally, but they can learn a great deal from the logic behind it.

item.title: Bionic FMCW LiDAR and the Rise of Adaptive 4D Machine Vision
item.description: Much of the excitement around lidar focuses on one number: resolution. But the more interesting question is where the system spends its resolution budget. That is what makes recent work on gaze-enabled FMCW lidar so important. Instead of trying to scan every direction with the same density, the system concentrates high-resolution sensing where it matters most.

item.title: Radar Basics: Mechanical Scan, Phased Array, AESA, and Over-the-Horizon Detection
item.description: Radar is often described as if it were mysterious or purely military. In reality, its basic logic is straightforward: send energy, wait for the echo, and calculate what the return tells you about distance, direction, speed, or movement.

item.title: High-Power Microwave Counter-UAS Systems: Where They Fit in Layered Defense
item.description: High-power microwave counter-UAS systems attract attention because they promise a non-kinetic way to disrupt electronics rather than physically intercept a target. That promise is strategically important, but it is often described too narrowly. The system is not the whole solution. It is only one part of a layered chain.

item.title: Frontier Radar Technologies: What Is Real, What Is Emerging, and What to Watch
item.description: Radar research headlines can be misleading because they mix together technologies that are shipping now, technologies that are entering early operational use, and ideas that are still fundamentally research-stage. For engineers, buyers, and program planners, that mix is dangerous. It creates confusion about what can be deployed this year and what should only shape longer-term roadmap thinking.

item.title: Infrared Modules
item.description: Infrared modules are selected for stable thermal imaging performance in challenging weather and low-visibility environments.

item.title: Security Equipment Integration
item.description: Security equipment integration helps customers connect existing field devices and third-party systems into Horizon so the final operation does not remain divided across separate interfaces. The service covers device inventory, protocol mapping, event normalization, control coordination, and commissioning support.

item.title: Laser Ranging Modules
item.description: Laser ranging modules support high-precision distance capture for tracking logic, warning thresholds, and multi-sensor fusion workflows.

item.title: Wireless Transmission Modules
item.description: Wireless modules provide low-latency telemetry and video backhaul options across temporary, remote, and distributed project sites.

item.title: Cyrentis Shares Its 2026 New Year Message
item.description: Cyrentis shared its 2026 New Year message with thanks to customers, project partners, suppliers, and collaborators who supported the company during its first stage of development.

item.title: Cyrentis Signs a New Electro-Optical Equipment Contract
item.description: Cyrentis announced in December 2025 that it had signed a new customer contract for electro-optical equipment supply and project support, marking another step in moving the SOC product family from product definition into actual delivery activity.

item.title: Cyrentis Expands Horizon with Fusion, Command, Archive, and Link
item.description: Following the public introduction of Horizon Sense, Cyrentis announced the next visible set of Horizon platform components in May 2025, extending the software direction from device management into operational workflow.

item.title: Cyrentis Formally Established and Introduces Horizon Sense
item.description: Wuhan Cyrentis Technology Co., Ltd. announced its formal establishment on April 1, 2025, with a business focus on integrated monitoring systems built around software, sensing products, and deployment-oriented engineering services.

item.title: Contact
item.description: Share your project scope, site conditions, sensing priorities, and integration expectations. Cyrentis can respond with product suggestions, solution architecture direction, or a more structured project discussion based on your scenario.

item.title: SRC-C03A-1G
item.description: SRC-C03A-1G Surveillance Radar is positioned for land border surveillance, critical site protection, and perimeter monitoring projects where operators need C band Pulse Doppler sensing and a readable site-warning layer. The electronic azimuth scanning architecture supports structured deployment planning and downstream response coordination.

item.title: SRC-K01B-2D
item.description: SRC-K01B-2D Surveillance Radar is positioned for border surveillance, maritime surveillance, and key site protection projects where operators need Ku band FMCW sensing and a readable site-warning layer. The mechanical azimuth scanning + electronic elevation steering architecture supports structured deployment planning and downstream response coordination.

item.title: SRC-K02E-2G
item.description: SRC-K02E-2G moving target surveillance radar is positioned for land border surveillance, maritime surveillance, and critical site protection projects where operators need Ku band FMCW sensing and a readable site-warning layer. The four-face aesa array architecture supports structured deployment planning and downstream response coordination.

item.title: SRC-K03B-2D
item.description: SRC-K03B-2D Surveillance Radar is positioned for border surveillance, maritime surveillance, and critical site protection projects where operators need Ku band FMCW sensing and a readable site-warning layer. The mechanical azimuth scanning + electronic elevation steering architecture supports structured deployment planning and downstream response coordination.

item.title: SRC-K04A-2G
item.description: SRC-K04A-2G Surveillance Radar is positioned for land border surveillance, maritime surveillance, and critical site protection projects where operators need Ku band FMCW sensing and a readable site-warning layer. The electronic azimuth scanning architecture supports structured deployment planning and downstream response coordination.

item.title: SRC-K05C-1D
item.description: SRC-K05C-1D Surveillance Radar is positioned for low-altitude surveillance, critical site protection, and counter-uas early warning projects where operators need Ku band Pulse Doppler sensing and a readable site-warning layer. The 2d electronic scanning (single-face aesa) architecture supports structured deployment planning and downstream response coordination.

item.title: SRC-K05D-1D
item.description: SRC-K05D-1D Surveillance Radar is positioned for low-altitude surveillance, critical site protection, and counter-uas early warning projects where operators need Ku band Pulse Doppler sensing and a readable site-warning layer. The electronic scanning with mechanical rotation architecture supports structured deployment planning and downstream response coordination.

item.title: SRC-K05E-1D
item.description: SRC-K05E-1D Surveillance Radar is positioned for low-altitude surveillance, critical site protection, and counter-uas early warning projects where operators need Ku band Pulse Doppler sensing and a readable site-warning layer. The four-face aesa array architecture supports structured deployment planning and downstream response coordination.

item.title: SRC-X01C-1D
item.description: SRC-X01C-1D Surveillance Radar is positioned for low-altitude surveillance, critical site protection, and counter-uas early warning projects where operators need X band Pulse Doppler sensing and a readable site-warning layer. The 2d electronic scanning (single-face aesa) architecture supports structured deployment planning and downstream response coordination.

item.title: SRC-X01D-1D
item.description: SRC-X01D-1D Surveillance Radar is positioned for low-altitude surveillance, critical site protection, and counter-uas early warning projects where operators need X band Pulse Doppler sensing and a readable site-warning layer. The electronic scanning with mechanical rotation architecture supports structured deployment planning and downstream response coordination.

The mechanical azimuth scanning + electronic elevation steering architecture supports structured deployment planning and downstream response coordination.
